coredata.dc:description: In the realm of efficient agricultural production, the conversion of large volumes of agroforestry waste into sustainable, high-value materials is imperative to address pollution, curb carbon dioxide emissions, and alleviate the burden of costly waste disposal. This study endeavored to address these challenges by concurrently synthesizing carbon quantum dots fluorescent nanomaterials and hydrothermal biochar through hydrothermal carbonization (HTC), employing corn straw (CS) as the primary precursor material. The synthesized CS-based carbon quantum dots (CS-CDs) exhibited a typical graphite structure, with a uniform particle size distribution and a particle size average of 2.28 ± 0.5 nm. Notably, CS-CDs displayed excitation-dependent photoluminescence (PL) behavior, remarkable water solubility, high fluorescence stability, and specific recognition capabilities for Fe3+ detection. The detection range of CS-CDs for Fe3+ spanned from 50 to 2000 μM, with a limit of detection (LOD) of 5.32 μM. Meanwhile, during the HTC process, the solid fraction yielded hydrothermal biochar (HC) at a rate of 57.60 %. HC was enriched with essential elements and held promise as an organic carbon fertilizer or premium-quality fuel, thereby realizing the multifaceted utilization potential of CS resources. This study offers valuable insights and innovative strategies for the high-value and multifunctional utilization of agroforestry resources, contributing to the promotion of sustainable agricultural practices.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Structural characterization of CS-CDs: TEM (a) and (b), SEM (c), size distribution histogram (d), XRD pattern (e), and Raman spectrum (f) of CS-CDs.

Structural characterization of CS-CDs: XPS survey spectrum (a), high-resolution C1s XPS spectrum (b), high-resolution O1s XPS spectrum (c), high-resolution N1s XPS spectrum (d), high-resolution P2p XPS spectrum (e) of CS-CDs, and FTIR spectrum (f) of CS and CS-CDs.

Optical characterization of CS-CDs: UV–vis absorption spectra (a), PL excitation and emission spectra (b), Excitation-emission PL spectra (c) of CS-CDs, CIE coordinates (d), Effect of UV irradiation time (e), ionic strength (f), pH (g), storage time (i) of CS-CDs obtained at 330 nm excitation, and the pH of CS-CDs changes with storage time (h).

Detection of Fe3+: Changes in the PL intensity ratio I/I0 of CS-CDs after the addition of different metal ions with the same concentration (a), Changes in the PL intensity ratio I/I0 of CS-CDs after the addition of different concentrations of Fe3+ ions (b). Linear fitting of the CS-CDs PL intensity vs Fe3+ concentration (c).
Characterization of HC: FTIR spectrum (a) of CS and HC, The adsorption-desorption isotherm (b) of HC, TGA (c), and DTG (d) curves for CS and HC.
Comparison of Various Reported CDs Utilized for Fe3+ Detection.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In the realm of efficient agricultural production, the conversion of large volumes of agroforestry waste into sustainable, high-value materials is imperative to address pollution, curb carbon dioxide emissions, and alleviate the burden of costly waste disposal. This study endeavored to address these challenges by concurrently synthesizing carbon quantum dots fluorescent nanomaterials and hydrothermal biochar through hydrothermal carbonization (HTC), employing corn straw (CS) as the primary precursor material. The synthesized CS-based carbon quantum dots (CS-CDs) exhibited a typical graphite structure, with a uniform particle size distribution and a particle size average of 2.28 ± 0.5 nm. Notably, CS-CDs displayed excitation-dependent photoluminescence (PL) behavior, remarkable water solubility, high fluorescence stability, and specific recognition capabilities for Fe3+ detection. The detection range of CS-CDs for Fe3+ spanned from 50 to 2000 μM, with a limit of detection (LOD) of 5.32 μM. Meanwhile, during the HTC process, the solid fraction yielded hydrothermal biochar (HC) at a rate of 57.60 %. HC was enriched with essential elements and held promise as an organic carbon fertilizer or premium-quality fuel, thereby realizing the multifaceted utilization potential of CS resources. This study offers valuable insights and innovative strategies for the high-value and multifunctional utilization of agroforestry resources, contributing to the promotion of sustainable agricultural practices.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: In the past few years, as China's grain production has steadily increased, the volume of agricultural solid waste has also escalated, with straw resources for 20 % of the global total (Fukase and Martin., 2020; Li et al., 2024). Despite proactive measures by the Chinese government to encourage the resourceful use of agricultural waste, challenges such as underutilization, steep processing costs, and the low-value addition of the products derived from comprehensive utilization persist (Asim et al., 2015; Zhang et al., 2020). Presently, the treatment of corn straw (CS) through its return to the field stands as a primary practice in China, aimed at enriching the crop environment. However, the inherent properties of CS, marked by high concentrations of lignin, cellulose and hemicellulose, along with a high carbon-to-nitrogen ratio, slow its decomposition process (Cui et al., 2022; Li et al., 2024). Thus, there exists a significant opportunity for the high-value utilization of CS, provided its applicability can be effectively demonstrated. By simplifying the transformation of these agricultural solid wastes into valuable materials, their utilization can be maximized, offering a sustainable solution to the challenges currently faced in agricultural waste management. The widespread availability of CS makes it an ideal raw material for producing CDs on a global scale. This accessibility in rural and peri-urban areas across developed and developing countries, irrespective of geographical constraints, streamlines the large-scale production of CDs, bolstering their application and development across various fields.

Carbon quantum dots (CDs), an innovative type of luminescent carbon nanoparticles, have garnered increasing interest since their serendipitous discovery by Xu et al. (2004) during the electrocatalytic process of carbon nanotubes. Subsequently, Sun et al. (2006) formally coined the term "carbon quantum dots". These CDs exhibit exceptional optical properties, rendering them highly suitable for diverse applications such as imaging, sensing, catalysis, and various optoelectronic devices (Ding et al., 2022; Fan et al., 2022; Jin et al., 2019; Liu et al., 2023).

With the widespread development of preparing CDs from biomass resources in recent years, there has been a growing number of reports on using CDs' fluorescence quenching effect to detect heavy metal ions. Yang et al. (2021) prepared CDs from CS using a solvent-based method for Cu2+ detection, which they suggested may be due to the faster chelation kinetics of Cu2+ compared to other ions and its higher affinity for oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface of CDs; Elango et al. (2022) derived CDs from crab shells for detecting Cd2+ ions, due to the strong interaction between CDs and Cd2+ ions; Goswami et al. (2022) demonstrated the selective detection of Cr6+ using banana stem-derived CDs, which they analyzed as being due to the fact that the transfer of electrons from CDs to Cr6+ leads to a significant decrease in potential and subsequent fluorescence quenching. Scholars propose that the applications of these CDs can vary significantly depending on the biomass carbon precursors used, resulting in distinctive chemical compositions of the synthesized CDs.

The synthesis of CDs predominantly employs both top-down and bottom-up approaches, with the latter being widely favored due to its simplicity, minimal setup requirements, and cost-effectiveness in practical applications. However, conventional methods often entail the use of pure reagents, including citric acid, ethylenediamine, urea, and glucose, among others (Ezati et al., 2022; Lei et al., 2022; Tang et al., 2021; Wen et al., 2020). Despite their efficacy, the utilization of chemical reagents incurs certain costs and may yield toxic byproducts, thereby posing environmental concerns. Presently, researchers have explored alternative routes for CDs synthesis, particularly via the hydrothermal method utilizing biomass wastes such as Poa pratensis, pine wood, Chinese herbs, cassava stem, rice husk, and others (Dhongde et al., 2024; Gao et al., 2023; Krishnaiah et al., 2022; Qiu et al., 2023; Zhao et al., 2020). These biomass residues, primarily composed of cellulose, hemicellulose, lignin, and other components, afford synthesized CDs exhibiting commendable biocompatibility and stability. Notably, agricultural solid wastes boasting similar compositions are abundantly available, renewable, and economically viable. Harnessing these resources for CDs production not only aligns with sustainability principles but also presents an opportunity to capitalize on their renewable, low-cost, abundant, and environmentally benign attributes. Thus, the exploration of synthesizing CDs from agricultural wastes holds promise for sustainable and eco-friendly nanomaterial production.

In the hydrothermal method of synthesizing CDs from biomass, the diverse array of species and intricate chemical composition inherent to biomass results in CDs with varying yields, fluorescence intensities, and properties under differing hydrothermal conditions. This variability poses a significant challenge to the real-time characterization of intermediates during the HTC process, impeding a comprehensive understanding of the mechanism behind CDs formation from these biomasses (Sta. et al., 2019; Wang et al., 2019a; Zhang et al., 2022). However, it is precisely due to these complex chemical compositions that gases, organic liquids, and solid products can be generated during the HTC of biomass for CDs preparation.

During the HTC process, biomass undergoes a series of transformations, including hydrolysis, dehydration, decarboxylation, aromatization, and recondensation, ultimately yielding HC (Lucian et al., 2019). Notably, the production of HC from biomass not only entails mild reaction conditions and a straightforward preparation process but also presents an environmentally friendly approach to mitigate pollution and address the energy crisis associated with traditional carbon sources such as coal and oil (Picone et al., 2024). Being rich in carbon content, HC finds versatile applications ranging from organic fertilizer and soil conditioner to solid fuel and biomedicine. This underscores its potential to contribute to sustainability initiatives across various sectors while offering a viable alternative to conventional carbon materials.

Iron ions (Fe3+), the most abundant essential transition metal ions, serve critical functions in living organisms (Liu et al., 2014). However, the uncontrolled release and accumulation of Fe3+ in the environment can yield adverse effects, particularly in contaminated water sources, thus compromising the growth of aquatic flora and fauna and disrupting ecosystem equilibrium (Ding et al., 2019; Liu et al., 2022). Presently, conventional Fe3+ detection techniques encompass plasma generation spectrometry and colorimetric methods, which necessitate sophisticated instrumentation and are time-intensive, expensive, and intricate in operation (Qi et al., 2019; Siahcheshm and Heiden., 2023). Consequently, there persists a pressing need to devise straightforward, environmentally friendly, sensitive, precise, and rapid detection methodologies for assessing Fe3+ levels in the environment. Fluorescent sensors with strong selectivity, high sensitivity, and fast response have been utilized for the identification of metal ions in the environment (Wu et al., 2017a; Zhang et al., 2014).

Therefore, opting to produce CDs from the vast and easily available waste material of CS would be a more favorable choice for the mass production of fluorescence quenching-based Fe3+ sensors. Despite the continuous emergence of CDs with varying sensing capabilities, their precise sensing mechanisms are still being actively researched. Challenges such as interference from other metal ions, the influence of environmental conditions on detection results, stability, and recyclability of CDs require further exploration and resolution. Additionally, improving the sensitivity of CDs to Fe3+, lowering detection limits, and expanding their application scope are pressing concerns that necessitate immediate attention in current research endeavors.

This study endeavored to utilize agricultural waste from CS to concurrently synthesize green fluorescent CS-CDs and HC with diverse application potentials. Firstly, the investigation focused on elucidating the structural and optical attributes of CS-CDs. Secondly, the metal ion sensing capabilities of CS-CDs, particularly focusing on Fe3+, were explored. Paper-based sensors were fabricated for Fe3+ detection to assess its practical applicability. Finally, a comprehensive analysis of HC was conducted, aiming to evaluate the viability of organic carbon fertilizer or biomass fuels. This research will provide new perspectives and practical strategies for the versatile and high-value utilization of agricultural byproducts.

The collected CS sourced from rural areas around Tianjin underwent a rigorous cleaning process involving multiple washes with tap water and deionized water, proceeding with drying at 60°C to constant weight. Subsequently, the CS was finely ground using a stainless steel pulverizer to pass through a 100-mesh screen and then stored in bags for future use. A predetermined quantity of CS powder was precisely weighed into a Teflon-lined autoclave, along with an appropriate proportion of ultrapure water, thoroughly mixed, and subjected to reaction at the specified temperature for a specified duration. After the reaction, the autoclave was left to cool naturally to room temperature. The resultant product was then subjected to centrifugation at 10,000 rpm for 20 minutes, followed by dual filtration using a 0.22 μm aqueous microporous membrane. The product obtained from carbonization was segregated into solid-phase carbon and liquid-phase carbon. The solid-phase carbon, known as HC, was dried to a constant weight at 105°C. Meanwhile, the liquid-phase carbon underwent purification using a 500 Da dialysis bag for 24 hours to obtain liquid CS-CDs. The liquid CS-CDs was subsequently freeze-dried to form a powder and kept below 4°C for later use.

This study employed a three-factor, three-level orthogonal experiment to optimize the synthesis process for both CS-CDs and HC. Specifically, the orthogonal experimental design was applied to three key parameters influencing HC production: hydrothermal time (set at 2, 5, and 10 h), hydrothermal temperature (set at 180, 200, and 220°C), and solid-liquid ratio (set at 1:5, 1:10, and 1:20). The L9 (33) orthogonal experimental design was utilized to investigate the fluorescence intensity of CS-CDs and the HC yield. The experimental scheme is detailed in Tables S1–2.

The separated liquid-phase carbon, which was not subjected to dialysis, underwent freeze-drying directly. After complete dissolution in an appropriate quantity of dichloromethane, the solution was filtered through a 0.22 μm organic-based microporous filter membrane. Following filtration, the composition was analyzed using gas chromatography-mass spectrometry (GC-MS). The results of this analysis are depicted in Figure S1 and summarized in Table S3.

Solutions containing various metal ions (K+, Na+, Fe3+, Ca2+, Co2+, Fe2+, Zn2+, Mg2+, Al3+, Cu2+, Mn2+) at a concentration of 250 μM were prepared with ultrapure water as the solvent to investigate the sensitivity of CS-CDs towards different metal ions. Subsequently, 1 ml of 50 μg/ml CS-CDs solution was mixed separately with each of the salt solutions and waited for 5 minutes before measuring the fluorescence intensity at the optimal excitation wavelength of 360 nm. Moreover, to determine the linear correlation between fluorescence intensity and ion concentration, selected metal ions were further tested at concentrations ranging from 0 to 2000 μM on CS-CDs. This comprehensive analysis aimed to elucidate the response of CS-CDs to different metal ions and their concentrations, providing insights into the selectivity and sensitivity of CS-CDs as metal ion sensors.

The commercially available cellulose filter paper was immersed into the CS-CDs solution, allowing the CS-CDs nanoparticles to adhere sufficiently. Afterward, the filter paper was removed and left to air dry at room temperature, resulting in the creation of a straightforward paper-based CS-CDs sensor. These paper-based sensors, coated with CS-CDs, were then immersed in Fe3+ solutions of varying concentrations (ranging from 0 to 1000 μM), left to stand for 10 minutes, and subsequently placed under 365 nm UV light for observation of the results.

The surface morphology of CS-CDs was examined using transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (F200 high-resolution transmission electron microscope, Electron Corporation, Japan) and scanning electron microscopy (SEM) (ZEISS sigma500, Zeiss, Germany). The phase structure of the CS-CDs was determined via X-ray diffractometry (XRD) (X′Pert Pro, Panacor, The Netherlands), while Raman spectroscopy was conducted at a wavelength of 532 nm (inVia Reflex, Renishaw, UK). Furthermore, the composition and bonding configurations of CS-CDs were determined using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) (ESCALAB 250Xi K-Alpha, Thermo Fisher, USA). Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) (INVENIO S, Bruker, GER) covered a scanning range of 4000–400 cm−1, while UV–visible absorption spectroscopy (MAPADA P3, China) spanned a range of 200–600 nm. Fluorescence characterization was performed using a fluorescence spectrophotometer (FLUORO 9 PLUS, China), and the pH meter (PB-10, Sartorius, Germany) was utilized to measure the pH value of the CS-CDs. Elemental analyses of HC were conducted on a fully automated elemental analyzer (Vario EL Ⅲ, Elementar, Germany), while the determination of metal ions was carried out using inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS; Agilent 7700, USA). Combustion characterization of HC was executed using a simultaneous thermal analyzer (TG) (STA449, NETZSCH, Germany), and specific surface area analysis (BET) was carried out using a specific surface area analyzer (ASAP 2460, Micromeritics, USA). Industrial analysis (SX-ES07123, ZHONGYIGUOKE, China) was conducted using the GBT method 28731–2012. Additionally, the high heating value (HHV) of HC was calculated using the formula (Katnić et al., 2022): HHV = 0.3491 C + 1.1783 H + 0.105 S - 0.1034 O - 0.0151 N - 0.0211 A. The total nutrient content of HC was determined, with N content obtained by elemental analyzer, P content determined by ammonium molybdate spectrophotometry (Yu et al., 2022), and K content measured by ICP-MS.

The tar components were analyzed via gas chromatography-mass spectrometry (GC-MS) (GCMS-QP2020 NX, SHIMADZU, Japan) in accordance with the procedure outlined by Zhang et al. (2021). The carrier gas used was helium at a flow rate of 1 ml/min. The temperature was initially held at 50°C for 5 minutes, then increased to 150°C at 8 °C/min. Subsequently, it is raised at 5 °C/min to 200°C for 5 minutes, followed by a further increase at 10 °C/min to 280°C for 8 minutes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Calculation of quantum yield of CS-CDs
ce:section.ce:para:
The quantum yield (QY) of CS-CDs was determined by the reference method relative to a benchmark value of quinine sulfate in 0.1 M H2SO4 solution (with a known QY of 54 %). Appropriate concentrations of quinine sulfate and CS-CDs solutions were prepared, and their absorbance values were measured using a UV–visible spectrophotometer. Care was taken to ensure that the absorbance remained below 0.1 to prevent the reabsorption effect caused by excessively high concentrations, which could lead to fluorescence bursts (Xie et al., 2019). Subsequently, the fluorescence emission peak areas of both quinine sulfate and CS-CDs were determined using a fluorescence spectrophotometer. The absorbance values of all solutions were measured at 360 nm. The relative QY was calculated as shown in expressions (1) and (2) (Ren et al., 2022):
(1)
QY

=
QY

A CDs

(2)

k CDs

) (

)
2

In the formula, QY represents the quantum yield, A represents the absorbance at the optimal excitation wavelength of 360 nm, I represents the integrated fluorescence intensity, η represents the refractive index of the solvent, k corresponds to the slopes of the linear regression curves of the integrated fluorescence intensity-absorption at different concentrations, and CDs and ST denote CS-CDs and quinine sulfate, respectively. where η CDs/η ref = 1.0.

In the case of CS-CDs, the solid-liquid ratio had the most significant impact on the HTC process, followed by the hydrothermal temperature, with hydrothermal time having the least significant influence. More precisely, the optimal parameters were identified: a hydrothermal temperature of 220°C, a hydrothermal duration of 10 hours, and a solid-liquid ratio of 1:5 (Table S4–5). For HC, the hydrothermal temperature exerted the most significant influence on the HTC process, followed by the solid-liquid ratio, with hydrothermal time having the least impact. The optimal conditions were identified: a hydrothermal temperature of 180°C, a hydrothermal duration of 2 hours, and a solid-liquid ratio of 1:5 (Table S6–7). Given that the fluorescence intensity of CS-CDs was the central point of interest in this research, the chosen optimal parameters included a hydrothermal temperature of 220°C and a solid-liquid ratio of 1:5. Although the impact of hydrothermal time on the fluorescence intensity of CS-CDs was marginal, a duration of 5 h was selected, considering factors like HC yield and cost-efficiency. In summary, the optimal process combination was determined to be a hydrothermal temperature of 220°C, hydrothermal time of 5 h, and solid-liquid ratio of 1:5. Subsequent tests were conducted using these parameters.

The surface morphology of CS-CDs synthesized from CS was examined using TEM and SEM. As illustrated in Fig. 1(a-c), TEM images revealed a uniform distribution of CS-CDs with clear and distinct lattice spacing, indicating a high degree of crystallinity and formation of a single continuous crystalline phase. The lattice stripe spacing of 0.21 nm observed corresponded to the (100) plane in a typical graphite structure (Atchudan et al., 2020b; Pu et al., 2020). SEM images depicted CS-CDs as uniform quasi-spherical particles, albeit with some aggregation and irregular clustering. Analysis of 300 CS-CDs yielded an average particle size of 2.28 ± 0.5 nm, with sizes ranging from 0.84 to 4.7 nm, as shown in Fig. 1(d). These homogeneous organic aggregates were formed through primary dehydration, cross-linking, and polymerization of CS. The resulting CS-CDs exhibited excellent water solubility, yielding a stable, yellow-brown, transparent aqueous solution even after several months of centrifugal filtration.

The phase structure and crystallinity of the synthetic CS-CDs were analyzed using XRD, with a representative wide-angle XRD image displayed in Fig. 1(e). A prominent diffraction peak centered at 22.8° was observed, corresponding to the (002) lattice spacing of the carbon-based material (Chen et al., 2023). This peak alignment corroborated the findings from TEM images, indicating a close interlayer stacking within the synthetic CS-CDs, resembling a graphite structure. It suggested that the tight stacking of highly conjugated sp2 structural domains predominates within the core of the CS-CDs, while functional groups or sp3 C widen the interlayer distance at the edges (Miao et al., 2017).

Raman spectra of CS-CDs, presented in Fig. 1(f), typically exhibit two common bands below 2000 cm−1: the disorder-induced D-band and the graphitic G-band. The D-band indicated the level of crystalline imperfections and disorder in graphite, whereas the G-band signified the vibration of sp2-bonded hybridized carbon atoms within the two-dimensional hexagonal lattice of graphite (Chen et al., 2022). The D-band associated with the disorder peaks around 1366 cm−1, whereas the G-band peaks at approximately 1530 cm−1. The graphitization degree of CDs was often defined by the ratio of peak intensity of G-band and D-band, IG/lD, where a higher ratio indicated a greater graphitization degree. The IG/lD values of CS-CDs were approximately 0.93, indicating an increasing presence of sp2 conjugation domains, which aligns with the TEM and XRD characterization findings.

Furthermore, the surface functional groups and elemental composition of the synthetic CS-CDs were characterized and analyzed using XPS and FTIR. XPS was pivotal in examining the composition and bonding configuration of CDs. Fig. 2(a-e) displayed both the full-scan and high-resolution XPS spectra of CS-CDs. In Fig. 2(a), the C1s, O1s, and N1s peaks of CS-CDs were observed at 285.08, 400.08, and 532.08 eV, respectively, with trace P also observed at 133.08 eV. The detailed XPS spectra of C (Fig. 2(b)) displayed three peaks at 284.88, 285.93, and 288.08 eV in the C1s region, suggesting the existence of sp2 C (C-C/CC), sp3 C (C-O), and CO bonds (Zhang et al., 2023a). This suggested that the surface of the CS-CDs was characterized by a large number of graphite structures and abundant chemical groups. Furthermore, the detailed XPS spectrum of O (Fig. 2(c)) showed three distinct peaks at 531.78, 532.32, and 533.73 eV, which can be attributed to CO, C-O-C/C-C-OH/P-O, and COOH, respectively (Hu et al., 2020; Ren et al., 2022). The detailed XPS spectra of N (Fig. 2(d)) exhibited two peaks at 399.81 and 401.18 eV, corresponding to graphitic N and amino N, respectively, providing evidence for the existence of elemental N bonded to the surface of CS-CDs (Nie et al., 2014). Additionally, a signal peak corresponding to P-O appeared at 132.93 eV in the P high-resolution XPS pattern (Fig. 2(e)) (Ren et al., 2022). These XPS spectra provided evidence of the existence of N, O, and P elements in the HTC of CS for the synthesis of CS-CDs. This composition enhanced the stability and hydrophilicity of CS-CDs in water, facilitating their further functionalization and practical application.

FTIR was used for further characterization of the surface functional groups of both CS and CS-CDs, as depicted in Fig. 2(f). Generally, CS and CS-CDs possess comparable surface functional groups, with only minor differences in relative intensities. Both CS and CS-CDs displayed a broad peak indicative of carboxylate groups in the range of 3500–2500 cm−1. Additionally, both materials exhibited alkane stretching vibration peaks. CS featured two prominent peaks at 2925 and 3315 cm−1, which are indicative of potential O-H and C-H bending vibrations (Hu et al., 2020). The 1638 cm−1 peak might be associated with either the amide I band or C-N stretching vibration, while the secondary amide II band was detected within the range of 1548–1529 cm−1 (Jian et al., 2017). The peaks observed between 1283 and 1529 cm−1 could potentially correspond to the C-N stretching vibration of aromatic ammonia (Travlou et al., 2018). For CS-CDs, the presence of peaks at 1615, 1548–1529, 1375, and 1318 cm−1 suggested the development of an aryl ammonia structure, suggesting the presence of a graphene-enriched sp2 structure in CS-CDs, consistent with TEM results (Liu et al., 2023). Notably, peak shifts were observed in CS-CDs compared to CS, likely due to the formation of aromatic structures. For instance, the band observed at 1048 cm−1 was likely associated with C-O stretching vibration (Hu et al., 2020). Furthermore, the N-H stretching vibrational summit at 952 cm−1 in CS-CDs indicated the presence of amino groups, consistent with previous findings (Liu et al., 2023). Additionally, changes in the C-O-C stretching vibration absorption peak in the range of 1188–1272 cm−1 may correspond to structural modifications in lignin during the HTC process (Zhao et al., 2020).

The optical characteristics of CS-CDs were investigated through PL and UV-Vis absorption spectroscopy. The UV-Vis absorption spectroscopy of CS-CDs in a water medium was depicted in Fig. 3(a). A peak was detected at 275 nm, indicating the π-π* transition of the carbon nucleus CC(sp2), suggesting the existence of conjugated double bonds and carbonyl groups on the surface of CS-CDs, corroborating the findings from FTIR analysis (Goswami et al., 2022). Notably, the CS-CDs solution appeared tan under normal daylight but exhibited vibrant blue luminescence under ultraviolet light with a wavelength of 365 nm (Fig. 3a, inset). The findings indicated that the ideal excitation wavelength for CS-CDs was 360 nm, and the optimal emission wavelength was 440 nm (Fig. 3(b)). The fluorescence emission of CS-CDs demonstrated excitation-dependent behavior.

Fig. 3(c) illustrated the excitation-dependent photoluminescence of CS-CDs, with the emission center experiencing a redshift from 415 nm to 515 nm as the excitation wavelength varied between 300 nm and 400 nm. The dependence-induced PL behavior observed in these CS-CDs particles during the hydrothermal process could be attributed to variations in the size and/or surface functional groups present (Goswami et al., 2022). On the one hand, for CDs primarily composed of a carbon core, the inhomogeneity of the particle size distribution affects the final optical properties of CDs by influencing the variation of the bandgap (Xu et al., 2021). On the other hand, during the HTC process, oxygen-containing functional groups appearing on the CS-CDs surface can introduce trapping sites with different energy levels, forming trapping centers for defective sites on the CS-CDs surface. Specific excitation wavelengths are conducive to activating certain emission trap sites, thereby resulting in excitation-dependent emissions (Hu et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2019b).

The relative QY parameter of CS-CDs, calculated with quinine sulfate as a standard, was about 3.8 %. In this study, the relative QY was not high in comparison to other reported CDs from biomass. Some researchers have synthesized CDs using apple leaves (Ren et al., 2022), peach leaves (Ren et al., 2023), watermelon juice (Lu et al., 2018) and flax straw (Hu et al., 2020) with QYs of 8.8 %, 7.71 %, 10.6 % and 8.4 %, respectively. This is most likely due to the presence of less N during the synthesis of CS-CDs (Li et al., 2023). The current research indicated that the N content present in synthesized CDs can influence the quantum yield of the CDs, wherein an increased N content corresponds to a higher quantum yield (Liao et al., 2016; Zheng et al., 2021). Furthermore, Wang et al. (2021) believed that extending the hydrothermal reaction time will result in the gradual dehydration and carbonization of the precursor, which can, to some extent, promote the formation and growth of carbon nuclei, thereby enhancing the QY of CDs. Considering the economic benefits of HC yield and cost, this study chose a relatively short hydrothermal time of 5 hours for hydrothermal treatment, which may also be a reason for the lower QY of the synthesized CS-CDs. Furthermore, the CS-CDs were characterized using the International Commission on Illumination (CIE) coordinated (0.1967, 0.1415) based on the blue fluorescence emission data under 360 nm excitation (Fig. 3d).

In assessing the broad applicability of CDs, their fluorescence stability emerged as a crucial consideration. Therefore, the study meticulously evaluated the fluorescence stability of CS-CDs under various conditions, including prolonged UV lamp irradiation, varying ionic strength, pH levels, and storage duration, along with the pH alteration during storage. The corresponding results were depicted in Fig. 3(e-i). After being continuously irradiated with 365 nm UV light for 7 hours, the fluorescence intensity of CS-CDs decreased by only 35 %, and it remained virtually unchanged upon further UV light exposure (Fig. 3(e)). The PL strength remained stable even at concentrations of up to 1.0 M NaCl (Fig. 3(f)) and throughout storage for approximately 60 days (Fig. 3(i)), with no significant alteration in pH observed during long-term storage (Fig. 3(h)). Moreover, as depicted in Fig. 3(g), CS-CDs exhibited consistent PL strength across pH values ranging from 3 to 12. However, at extreme pH values (>12 or <3), a noticeable decrease in PL strength occurred, possibly attributable to chemical reactions of surface functional groups via strong intermolecular hydrogen bonding (Guo et al., 2020). These findings underscored the robust stability of synthetic CS-CDs, characterized by resistance to ionic strength, photobleaching, and maintenance of fluorescence intensity over extended durations. Such stability paved the way for cost-effective, large-scale production of CS-CDs derived from agricultural waste, facilitating their diverse practical applications.

Synthetic CS-CDs exhibited robust fluorescence properties, excellent stability, water solubility, and an abundance of surface functional groups, making them highly promising sensing materials. Notably, the oxygen functional groups on the CS-CDs' surface can interact with metal ions, forming complexes or chelates via ligand effects, potentially leading to fluorescence bursts (Wang et al., 2017; Zhai et al., 2022). Hence, the feasibility of CS-CDs as metal ion detectors in aqueous environments was systematically explored.

This study investigated the effects of 11 common metal ions (K+, Na+, Fe3+, Ca2+, Co2+, Fe2+, Zn2+, Mg2+, Al3+, Cu2+, and Mn2+) on the fluorescence intensity of CS-CDs solutions under 360 nm light excitation. The bar graph in Fig. 4(a) compared the fluorescence intensities (I/I0, where I is the fluorescence intensity of CS-CDs with metal ions, while I0 represents the fluorescence intensity of CS-CDs without metal ions) after adding several metal ions. It was apparent that Fe3+ induced a notable quenching effect on the fluorescence emitted by CS-CDs in contrast to the other metal ions, while the impact of other metal ions was less pronounced. This suggested that CS-CDs had a higher affinity for Fe3+ and undergo a faster chelation process, with most other metal ions having minimal interference in Fe3+ detection. The fluorescence quenching effect of Fe3+ on CS-CDs may arise from Fe3+ chelation with oxygen (or nitrogen) containing groups on the CS-CDs' surface, forming non-luminous complexes. This disrupted the radiative excitation, resulting in a fluorescence burst of the original CS-CDs (Chen et al., 2018; Zhao et al., 2020). These findings underscored the highly selective and stable response of synthetic CS-CDs for detecting Fe3+ in aqueous solutions.

Additionally, the sensitivity of CS-CDs in detecting Fe3+ was crucial for its practical application. To establish the detection range, the fluorescence intensity changes of CS-CDs solutions were investigated upon the addition of varying concentrations of Fe3+. The corresponding results were presented in Fig. 4(b). Remarkably, there was a discernible trend observed in the fluorescence intensity of CS-CDs as the concentration of Fe3+ in the solution increased, indicating the sensitivity of CS-CDs to different Fe3+ concentrations.

As depicted in Fig. 4(c), a robust linear correlation was observed between the concentration of Fe3+ and the intensity of fluorescence, represented by the following equations: I/I0 = −0.0006 C + 0.4706 (50 ∼ 400 μM, R2=0.9927), and I/I0 = −0.00004 C + 0.2538 (400 ∼ 2000 μM, R² = 0.9913). The LOD was calculated to be 5.32 μM (LOD = 3 σ/s, where σ is the standard deviation of the signal for n = 6, and s represents the slope of the linear calibration plot) (Atchudan et al., 2020a).

Comparatively, as shown in Table 1, CS-CDs synthesized from CS offered several advantages including a wide detection range, low LOD, rapid response, good recoverability, and convenient synthesis materials. They demonstrated excellent ability in detecting Fe3+ in water. Furthermore, the results of the paper-based sensor fabricated from cellulose filter paper were depicted in Figure S2. The sensor's fluorescence intensity declined as the Fe3+ solution concentration increased from 0 to 1000 μM under 365 nm UV irradiation. This method presented an effective and convenient approach for the qualitative detection of Fe3+ in target samples.

It is worth noting that this cost-effective and straightforward method not only yields CS-CDs but also offers an efficient and environmentally friendly approach for synthesizing HC. Throughout the HTC process, besides generating CS-CDs in the liquid-phase solution, HC was also produced in the solid-phase carbon fraction. HC primarily originated from the carbonization products resulting from the dehydration and decarboxylation reactions of cellulose-like substances present in CS. Generally, the intensity of the reaction determined the extent of decomposition of these cellulose-like structural substances, thereby impacting the yield of HC. In this experiment, the HC yield achieved was 57.60 %, with a total nutrient content (N+P2O5+K2O) of 3.606 % and a calculated higher heating value (HHV) of 17.99 MJ/kg.

Table 2 outlined the elemental and industrial analytical properties of HC derived from CS after hydrothermal treatment. Notably, the relatively low oxygen content in HC can be attributed to the dehydration reaction between hydrogen radicals and oxygen atoms during the HTC process. This reaction led to the transfer of oxygen from HC to the liquid phase, resulting in reduced volatile matter (Wu et al., 2017b). Excessive loss of volatile matter is typically correlated with increased ash content, while the amounts of fixed carbon and other residual minerals remain relatively stable. The elemental composition of HC was presented in Table S8. Following hydrothermal treatment, HC exhibited richness in metal elements, with sodium, magnesium, potassium, calcium, and iron, among others, being more abundant. These elements serve as essential nutrients for plant growth and can be assimilated by plants as organic fertilizer (Sigua et al., 2016). However, considering the potential risk of heavy metal contamination, it was essential to manage the heavy metal content to prevent soil pollution and ecotoxicity. As of now, there is no universally accepted international standard for the maximum allowable levels of nickel, copper, zinc, arsenic, cadmium, and lead in organic fertilizers (Thompson et al., 2020). Nevertheless, the concentrations of arsenic, lead, and cadmium in the HC obtained in this study fall below the safe thresholds stipulated by China's NY525–2021 (As≤15 mg/kg, Pb≤50 mg/kg, Cd≤3 mg/kg).

A comparison of the surface functional groups of HC and CS was done using FTIR analysis. The absorption peak at 3365 cm−1 in Fig. 5(a) was likely due to the stretching vibration of -OH groups. This peak exhibited decreased intensity in HC compared to CS, indicative of dehydration reactions occurring during the HTC process (Liang et al., 2019b). The absorption peaks between 2800 and 3000 cm−1 represented the C-H bond stretching vibrations in the aromatic structure. Furthermore, the sharp peak at 1619 cm−1 represented the CO bond stretching vibration, possibly associated with decarboxylation reactions during HTC. The peak around 1507 cm−1, related to CC stretching in lignocellulose aromatic groups, decreased with hydrothermal heating. Broadband around 1036 cm−1 signified the stretching vibration of C-O-C in cellulose, and hemicellulose is present in CS. The diminished intensity of this absorption peak may be attributed to the continuous decomposition and destruction of hemicellulose and cellulose structures during HTC, leading to the formation of HC, CS-CDs, and other liquid-phase compounds. The weakening of the absorption peak at 880 cm−1 in HC indicated a decrease in the intensity of the out-of-plane bending vibrational peak of the C-H bond within the aromatic ring. This suggested the occurrence of aromatization reactions during HTC (Sevilla and Fuertes, 2009).

The adsorption-resolving properties of HC were characterized using a BET analyzer, with the adsorption-desorption isotherms displayed in Fig. 5(b) showcasing typical type IV isotherms. Throughout the adsorption process, the N2 adsorption by HC increased with relative pressure, with a notable surge at very low pressures due to the presence of microporous regions (Chai et al., 2019). Moreover, the delayed return line of the sample at high relative pressures suggested the coexistence of micropores and mesopores in HC, indicating the presence of mesoporous regions (Qiu et al., 2021). Following the BET multi-point method, HC exhibited a specific surface area of 12.3668 m2/g, a total pore volume of 0.060314 cm3/g, and an average pore size of 19.5084 nm. Although smaller compared to common commercial activated carbons (300 ∼ 1000 m2/ g) (Rezma et al., 2017), this was greater than the pore volume and specific surface area of peanut shell biochar synthesized by Rohit Kushwaha et al. (2023) modified by KMnO4 and KOH.

Fig. 5(c) and (d) depicted the TG and DTG curves of HC, revealing a three-stage trend in the TG curve. Stage I reflected a slight decrease in sample mass, primarily due to moisture and volatile content, reducing the combustion activity of HC. Stage II exhibited a rapid decrease in sample mass, corresponding to the decomposition and combustion of cellulose post-HTC, leading to HC formation. Stage III signified a significant decrease in sample mass, primarily from the combustion of fixed carbon. Notably, CS displayed a higher weight loss rate in Stage III, potentially due to the formation of a more developed pore structure during sample volatilization, reducing heat transfer resistance during combustion (Liang et al., 2019a). Overall, the superior combustion stability of HC post-HTC suggested many possible uses for the versatile utilization of CS resources.

In summary, a straightforward one-step hydrothermal method was employed to extract fluorescent CS-CDs and HC from CS without any chemical reagents. Under optimal conditions, CS-CDs were synthesized with a relative quantum yield of 3.8 %, while HC was obtained with a yield of 57.60 %. The CS-CDs exhibited an average particle size of 2.28 ± 0.5 nm and displayed uniform distribution, showcasing excitation-dependent photoluminescence behavior, excellent fluorescence stability, and specific recognition capabilities for Fe3+ detection. The selective detection of Fe3+ by CS-CDs demonstrated a linear detection range of 50–2000 μM, with a LOD of 5.32 μM. Paper-based sensors fabricated using cellulose filter paper exhibited reduced fluorescence intensity with increasing Fe3+ concentration within the range of 0–1000 μM. Furthermore, the prepared HC, characterized by a total nutrient content (N+P2O5+K2O) of 3.606 % and a high HHV of 17.99 MJ/kg, held promise as an organic carbon fertilizer or a premium-quality fuel. This underscored the potential for the comprehensive utilization of CS resources across various applications.

Lan Mu: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Junyu Tao: Supervision, Conceptualization. Guanyi Chen: Supervision, Conceptualization. Hao Peng: Writing – review & editing. Yingjin Song: Writing – review & editing. Wanting Wu: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Conceptualization. Jialiang Huang: Visualization.
